architecture.summary: This is a position paper that does not propose a novel system. It analyzes existing sparse autoencoder (SAE) methods applied to language model representations. SAEs train an encoder-decoder pair on LM token activations: the encoder computes a sparse latent representation (only top-k dimensions nonzero) that reconstructs the original representation via the decoder. The paper argues SAEs excel at discovering unknown concepts (enumerating interpretable latent features that satisfy some criterion) rather than acting on known concepts (steering or probing for pre-specified concepts).

biblio.title: Position: Use Sparse Autoencoders to Discover Unknowns

claims.statement: SAEs may be less effective for acting on known concepts, but are especially powerful tools for discovering unknown concepts.

baseline.name: logistic regression on LM representations; prompting; finetuning; bag-of-words

claims.dataset: 113 binary classification tasks (Kantamneni et al. 2025); 500 synthetic concept tasks (Wu et al. 2025)
claims.statement: Negative SAE results in concept detection and model steering show that classifiers trained on SAE latent representations do not outperform classifiers trained directly on language model representations.

baseline.name: text embedding features; LLM prompting; topic models; n-grams

claims.dataset: news headline engagement; Congressional speech partisanship
claims.statement: In hypothesis generation on news headlines and Congressional speeches, SAE-derived hypotheses about concepts predicting engagement outperform hypotheses generated by text embedding feature selection, LLM prompting, topic modeling, or n-grams.

SAE features trained on language model representations yield monosemantic neurons that can be explained by a single concept, whereas language model neurons are polysemantic and encode multiple concepts at once.

In mechanistic explanation of LM outputs (biology of LLMs), SAEs reveal that language models represent specific task-relevant concepts: e.g., 'words rhyming with it' when composing rhyming couplets, and 'units digit 5' and 'addition problems of 40 plus 50' when performing arithmetic.
SAE reconstructions of LM representations encode strictly less information than the original LM representation, which explains why SAEs underperform on concept detection tasks.

SAEs can be applied to discover unknown concepts that bias LLM outputs in fairness/auditing tasks, to identify symptoms predicting clinical outcomes in health sciences, to trace language change in social sciences, and to close the prediction-explanation gap by converting black-box representations into interpretable ones.

Concept bottleneck models and other concept-based architectures require concepts to be prespecified before training (either by domain experts or language models), whereas SAE concepts are learned during training without prior specification.
